name: Alto de Velefique and Calar Alto - circular through the Sierra de los Filabres
name_original: Alto de Velefique y Calar Alto — circular por la Sierra de los Filabres
region_intro: Road cycling routes in Desierto De Tabernas traverse Europe's only true desert, characterized by its arid, "badlands" topography. The region features eroded ravines, dry riverbeds, and barren slopes, set against a backdrop of surrounding mountains like the Sierra de los Filabres and Sierra Alhamilla. This unique landscape offers significant elevation changes and challenging climbs, making it a distinctive destination for road cyclists. The terrain is shaped by millions of years of wind and rain, creating a visually striking environment. Best road cycling routes in Desierto De Tabernas The most popular road cycling route is Alto de Velefique and Calar Alto - circular through the Sierra de los Filabres, a 70.6 miles (113.6 km) trail that takes 6 hours 50 minutes to complete. This difficult route features substantial elevation gain, climbing towards high points like the Observatory at Calar Alto. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Climb to Alto de Velefique – Velefique climb loop from Cerro de Santiago, a difficult 53.1 miles (85.5 km) path. This route offers sustained climbing through mountainous terrain, including switchbacks characteristic of the Alto de Velefique. Local road cyclists also love the La Merendera Recreation Area – Venta Luisa Pass loop from Gérgal, a 28.0 miles (45.1 km) trail leading through arid landscapes and offering significant ascents, often completed in about 2 hours 59 minutes. Road cycling in Desierto De Tabernas is defined by arid "badlands" formations, challenging mountain passes, and eroded ravines. The network offers options for different ability levels, from moderate to very difficult. The routes in Desierto De Tabernas are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.7 stars from over 20 reviews. More than 1900 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Desierto De Tabernas's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many road cycling routes are available in Desierto de Tabernas?
A: Desierto de Tabernas offers a wide selection of road cycling routes, with over 45 options available. These routes cater to various skill levels, ranging from moderate to very difficult, ensuring there's a suitable challenge for most cyclists.

Q: What is the general difficulty level of road cycling in Desierto de Tabernas?
A: Road cycling in Desierto de Tabernas is generally challenging, characterized by significant elevation changes and mountainous terrain. While there are a few moderate routes, the majority, about 32 out of 46, are classified as difficult, making it an ideal destination for experienced cyclists seeking a demanding ride.

Q: Are there any beginner-friendly road cycling routes in Desierto de Tabernas?
A: While Desierto de Tabernas is known for its challenging terrain, there are a couple of routes considered easy, and 12 moderate options available. These routes offer a less strenuous introduction to the region's unique landscapes, though cyclists should still be prepared for some ascents.

Q: What kind of landscapes can I expect to see while road cycling in Desierto de Tabernas?
A: You'll experience Europe's only true desert, with dramatic 'badlands' topography featuring eroded ravines, dry riverbeds (ramblas), and barren slopes. The routes are set against a backdrop of mountains like the Sierra de los Filabres and Sierra Alhamilla, offering visually striking environments and panoramic views of the arid landscape and distant sea.

Q: Are there any notable landmarks or viewpoints along the road cycling routes?
A: Yes, many routes offer breathtaking panoramic views. You might encounter unique geological formations like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/7228798" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;The converging layers of earth&lt;/a&gt;, or stunning desert vistas such as &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/7236024" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;View of the Rambla&lt;/a&gt; and &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3564584" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Desert canyon views&lt;/a&gt;. The region is also famous for its film sets, like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3394954" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Fuerte El Cóndor Film Set Ruins&lt;/a&gt;, adding a unique cultural element to the natural scenery.

Q: What is the best time of year for road cycling in Desierto de Tabernas?
A: The best time for road cycling in Desierto de Tabernas is typically during the cooler months, from autumn through spring (October to May). Summers can be extremely hot, making long rides challenging. Spring offers pleasant temperatures and a chance to see some of the unique xerophytic vegetation in bloom.

Q: Are there any loop road cycling routes in Desierto de Tabernas?
A: Yes, many of the road cycling routes in Desierto de Tabernas are designed as loops. For example, the challenging &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e984700424/alto-de-velefique-and-calar-alto-circular-through-the-sierra-de-los-filabres" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Alto de Velefique and Calar Alto - circular through the Sierra de los Filabres&lt;/a&gt; is a popular loop, as is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20118795" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;La Merendera Recreation Area – Venta Luisa Pass loop from Gérgal&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What are some of the most challenging road cycling routes in the area?
A: For experienced cyclists seeking a significant challenge, routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e984700424/alto-de-velefique-and-calar-alto-circular-through-the-sierra-de-los-filabres" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Alto de Velefique and Calar Alto - circular through the Sierra de los Filabres&lt;/a&gt;, with its substantial elevation gain towards the Observatory at Calar Alto, or the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20118938" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Climb to Alto de Velefique – Velefique climb loop from Cerro de Santiago&lt;/a&gt;, known for its sustained climbing and switchbacks, are excellent choices.

Q: What do other road cyclists enjoy the most about road cycling in Desierto de Tabernas?
A: The area is highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.7 stars from over 20 reviews. Cyclists often praise the unique, almost lunar landscapes, the challenging mountain passes, and the smooth tarmac on many roads, which make for an enjoyable and memorable cycling experience.

Q: Are there any specific routes that feature the famous Alto de Velefique climb?
A: Absolutely. The Alto de Velefique is a highlight for many cyclists. You can experience it on routes such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20118938" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Climb to Alto de Velefique – Velefique climb loop from Cerro de Santiago&lt;/a&gt;, which offers sustained climbing and switchbacks reminiscent of Alpe d'Huez. Another option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31815793" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Climb to Alto de Velefique – Alto de Velefique loop from Gérgal&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What kind of road surfaces can I expect on the cycling routes?
A: Many of the roads in Desierto de Tabernas feature smooth tarmac, providing an enjoyable surface for road cycling. While the terrain is challenging with significant elevation changes, the quality of the road surface generally contributes to a pleasant riding experience.

Q: Can I find routes that combine desert landscapes with mountain views?
A: Yes, the region is uniquely positioned between the arid desert and surrounding mountain ranges like the Sierra de los Filabres. Routes often traverse the distinctive 'badlands' and then ascend into the mountains, offering a dynamic cycling experience with constantly changing scenery and breathtaking panoramic views that encompass both the desert and distant peaks.
tips: Olula de Castro is a town located at the foot of the Sierra de los Filabres, mountains with low vegetation but where the town is distinguished by the white walls of its houses.
Stunning climb, considered Spanish's "Alpe d'Huez" due to the similar length and switchbacks.
The Venta Luisa pass rises 1,970 meters above sea level and offers spectacular scenery. It can be climbed from Bacares, Serón or Gérgal.

The Calar Alto plateau is located at an altitude of 2,168 metres, in the heart of the Sierra de los Filabres. Its clear sky allows us to see the stars at night, which is why this peak houses the largest observatory in Europe.
